 {"id":"bd-gptu","title":"Implement rate-limit governor (adaptive concurrency)","description":"Task: Implement Rate-Limit Governor

Purpose
-------
Dynamically adjust parallelism based on real rate limit data from GitHub
API and model responses, preventing throttling and cascading failures.

Background: Why Adaptive?
-------------------------
Static parallelism is dangerous:
- Too high: hit rate limits, get blocked
- Too low: waste time when limits are fine
- Limits vary by time, account tier, prior usage

Solution: query real limits, adjust dynamically.

Governor State
--------------
GOVERNOR_STATE associative array:
- github_remaining: remaining API calls
- github_reset: when limit resets (epoch)
- model_in_backoff: currently backing off?
- model_backoff_until: when backoff ends
- effective_parallelism: current allowed sessions
- circuit_breaker_open: emergency stop?

Implementation
--------------

start_rate_limit_governor()
  Run in background during review:
  while review lock held:
    update_github_rate_limit
    check_model_rate_limit
    adjust_parallelism
    sleep 30

update_github_rate_limit()
  Query: gh api rate_limit
  Extract: .resources.core.remaining and .reset
  If remaining < 500: log warning, reduce parallelism

check_model_rate_limit()
  Scan session logs for 429/rate limit patterns
  If multiple recent hits:
    Set model_in_backoff = true
    Set backoff_until = now + 60 seconds

adjust_parallelism()
  Start with target = REVIEW_PARALLEL (default 4)
  
  If github_remaining < 1000: target /= 2
  If model_in_backoff: target = 1
  
  Count recent errors in sliding window
  If errors > 5 in 5 minutes:
    Open circuit breaker
    target = 0
    Log emergency pause
  
  Set effective_parallelism = target

can_start_new_session()
  Return false if:
  - circuit_breaker_open
  - model_in_backoff
  - active_sessions >= effective_parallelism
  Otherwise true

Circuit Breaker
---------------
Pattern for handling cascading failures:
- CLOSED: normal operation
- OPEN: too many failures, pause all
- HALF-OPEN: after cooldown, try one (not implemented yet)

Metrics Tracked
---------------
- API calls per minute
- 429 responses per session
- Session error rate
- Effective vs requested parallelism

Integration Points
------------------
- Called before starting new session
- Called in session monitor loop
- Updates TUI status display
- Writes metrics for analytics

Testing
-------
- Mock gh api rate_limit responses
- Simulate 429 responses
- Verify parallelism adjusts
- Verify circuit breaker triggers

Acceptance Criteria
-------------------
- [ ] Queries real GitHub rate limits
- [ ] Detects model rate limits from logs
- [ ] Adjusts parallelism dynamically
- [ ] Circuit breaker prevents cascading failures
- [ ] Metrics tracked for analysis","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-04T15:38:47.005795803-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-04T15:38:47.005795803-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-gptu","depends_on_id":"bd-jm89","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-04T16:17:00.313011296-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-hmw8","title":"Implement command validation and blocking","description":"Security: Validate commands before execution in agent sessions.

Three categories:

SAFE_BASH_COMMANDS (always allowed):
  git, grep, find, ls, cat, make, npm, cargo, go, python, pytest,
  shellcheck, eslint, prettier, jq, sed, awk, sort, diff

APPROVAL_REQUIRED_COMMANDS (human confirmation):
  rm, mv, cp (file operations)
  curl, wget (network)
  docker, kubectl (containers)

BLOCKED_COMMANDS (never allowed):
  sudo, su, chmod +x, chown, eval, exec

Special gh handling:
  READ allowed: gh issue view, gh pr view, gh issue list
  BLOCKED in Plan mode: gh issue comment, gh issue close, gh pr merge

validate_agent_command() checks command against lists, returns:
  0 = allowed
  1 = blocked
  2 = needs approval

Called by pre-exec hook in agent sessions.

Acceptance: Blocked commands rejected, approval commands queued, gh mutations blocked in Plan mode.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-04T15:43:25.152161697-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-04T15:43:25.152161697-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-hmw8","depends_on_id":"bd-9s7y","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-04T15:45:17.523931567-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-jen3","title":"Implement review command exit codes and error classification","description":"# Task: Implement Review Command Exit Codes

## Purpose
Define and implement consistent exit codes for `ru review` following the ru exit code conventions, enabling automation and scripting.

## Background: ru Exit Code Convention
From AGENTS.md, ru uses these exit codes:
- 0 = Success (all repos synced/reviewed)
- 1 = Partial failure (some repos failed)
- 2 = Conflicts exist (needs resolution)
- 3 = Dependency/system error (gh missing, auth failed)
- 4 = Invalid arguments
- 5 = Interrupted (use --resume)

## Review-Specific Exit Codes

### Exit 0: Success
- All sessions completed successfully
- All questions answered
- All approved changes applied (if --apply)

### Exit 1: Partial Failure
- Some sessions failed but others succeeded
- Some repos could not be reviewed (network, rate limit)
- Partial progress was made

### Exit 2: Conflicts/Review Needed
- Agent detected merge conflicts in worktree
- Quality gates failed (tests/lint)
- Changes require human intervention before apply

### Exit 3: Dependency/System Error
- Claude Code not installed or not in PATH
- gh CLI not authenticated
- tmux not available (for local driver)
- ntm not available (when --mode=ntm specified)

### Exit 4: Invalid Arguments
- Unknown flags
- Invalid --mode value
- Invalid --priority value
- Conflicting options (--plan with --push)

### Exit 5: Interrupted
- SIGINT/SIGTERM received
- --max-runtime exceeded
- --max-questions exceeded
- Checkpoint saved, use --resume to continue

## Implementation

### Error Classification Function
```bash
classify_review_error() {
    local error_type="$1"
    local context="$2"
    
    case "$error_type" in
        session_failed|rate_limited|network_error)
            echo "partial"  # Exit 1
            ;;
        merge_conflict|quality_gate_failed|tests_failed)
            echo "conflict"  # Exit 2
            ;;
        missing_dependency|auth_failed|no_driver)
            echo "system"  # Exit 3
            ;;
        invalid_flag|bad_mode|conflicting_options)
            echo "invalid"  # Exit 4
            ;;
        interrupted|timeout|max_questions)
            echo "interrupted"  # Exit 5
            ;;
        *)
            echo "unknown"
            ;;
    esac
}
```

### Exit Code Aggregation
```bash
aggregate_exit_code() {
    local -a exit_codes=("$@")
    local max_code=0
    
    for code in "${exit_codes[@]}"; do
        [[ $code -gt $max_code ]] && max_code=$code
    done
    
    # Special case: 5 (interrupted) takes precedence over 1 (partial)
    if [[ " ${exit_codes[*]} " =~ " 5 " ]]; then
        echo 5
        return
    fi
    
    echo $max_code
}
```

### Final Exit Handling
```bash
finalize_review_exit() {
    local exit_code="$1"
    
    case "$exit_code" in
        0) log_success "Review completed successfully" ;;
        1) log_warn "Review completed with partial failures" ;;
        2) log_error "Review blocked by conflicts - manual resolution needed" ;;
        3) log_error "Review failed due to system/dependency error" ;;
        4) log_error "Invalid arguments" ;;
        5) log_warn "Review interrupted - use --resume to continue" ;;
    esac
    
    exit "$exit_code"
}
```

## Testing
- Verify each exit code for its conditions
- Verify --resume works after exit 5
- Verify aggregation chooses worst code
- Verify CI can parse exit codes

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 6 exit codes defined and documented
- [ ] Error classification works correctly
- [ ] Exit code aggregation from multiple sessions
- [ ] Clear log message for each exit code
- [ ] --resume only available after exit 5

-{"id":"bd-jm89","title":"Define unified session driver interface","description":"# Task: Define Unified Session Driver Interface

## Purpose
Create an abstract interface that both ntm and local drivers implement, enabling graceful degradation and consistent behavior regardless of which driver is used.

## Background: Why a Unified Interface?
- ntm provides advanced orchestration but may not be installed
- Local driver (tmux + stream-json) works everywhere
- Same code should work with either driver
- Enables testing without full ntm dependency
- Future drivers (remote, cloud) can plug in

## Interface Definition

### Driver Capabilities
```bash
# Query what driver can do
driver_capabilities() {
    # Returns JSON with capabilities
    cat << EOF
{
  "name": "local",
  "parallel_sessions": true,
  "activity_detection": false,
  "health_monitoring": false,
  "question_routing": true
}
EOF
}
```

### Core Operations
```bash
# Start a new Claude session
# Args: worktree_path, session_name, initial_prompt
# Returns: session_id
driver_start_session() {
    local wt_path="$1"
    local session_name="$2"
    local prompt="$3"
    # Implementation varies by driver
}

# Send message/answer to session
# Args: session_id, message
# Returns: 0 on success
driver_send_to_session() {
    local session_id="$1"
    local message="$2"
}

# Get current session state
# Args: session_id
# Returns: JSON with state info
driver_get_session_state() {
    local session_id="$1"
    # Returns: {"state": "generating|waiting|complete|error", ...}
}

# Stop/kill a session
# Args: session_id
driver_stop_session() {
    local session_id="$1"
}

# Interrupt session (Ctrl+C equivalent)
# Args: session_id
driver_interrupt_session() {
    local session_id="$1"
}
```

### Event Streaming
```bash
# Stream events from session
# Args: session_id, callback_function
# Callback receives: event_type, event_data
driver_stream_events() {
    local session_id="$1"
    local callback="$2"
    # Calls: $callback "question" "$question_json"
    # Calls: $callback "complete" "$status"
    # Calls: $callback "error" "$error_msg"
}
```

### Normalized Event Schema
Both drivers emit the same event format:
```json
{
  "type": "init|generating|waiting|complete|error",
  "session_id": "...",
  "timestamp": "2025-01-04T10:30:00Z",
  "wait_info": {
    "reason": "ask_user_question|agent_question_text|external_prompt|unknown",
    "context": "...",
    "options": ["a) ...", "b) ..."],
    "recommended": "a",
    "risk_level": "low|medium|high"
  }
}
```

## Driver Selection
```bash
detect_review_driver() {
    # Try ntm first (preferred)
    if command -v ntm &>/dev/null; then
        if ntm --robot-status &>/dev/null 2>&1; then
            log_verbose "Using ntm driver (robot mode available)"
            echo "ntm"
            return
        fi
        log_verbose "ntm found but robot mode unavailable"
    fi

    # Fall back to local driver
    if command -v tmux &>/dev/null && command -v claude &>/dev/null; then
        log_verbose "Using local driver (tmux + stream-json)"
        echo "local"
        return
    fi

    log_error "No suitable driver available"
    echo "unsupported"
}
```

## Driver Loading
```bash
load_review_driver() {
    local driver="$1"
    
    case "$driver" in
        ntm)
            source "$RU_LIB_DIR/drivers/ntm.sh"
            ;;
        local)
            source "$RU_LIB_DIR/drivers/local.sh"
            ;;
        *)
            log_error "Unknown driver: $driver"
            return 1
            ;;
    esac
    
    REVIEW_DRIVER="$driver"
}
```

## Implementation Notes
- Functions prefixed with driver_ are implemented per-driver
- Common code calls driver_* functions
- Drivers can extend interface with driver-specific features
- Capabilities check enables graceful feature degradation

## Testing
- Mock driver for unit tests
- Verify interface consistency between drivers
- Test driver selection logic
- Verify event normalization

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Interface defined with all required operations
- [ ] Driver selection works correctly
- [ ] Event schema documented and consistent
- [ ] Capabilities query implemented
- [ ] Driver loading mechanism works","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-04T15:35:14.284949263-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-04T16:36:04.928160474-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-jm89","depends_on_id":"bd-mnu9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-04T16:08:16.453406735-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-k1kx","title":"Implement ntm driver using robot mode API","description":"Task: Implement ntm Driver Using Robot Mode API

Purpose
-------
Implement the unified session driver interface using ntm robot mode,
providing advanced orchestration capabilities beyond the local driver.

ntm Robot Mode API
------------------
ntm exposes a JSON API via command-line flags:

1. --robot-spawn SESSION_NAME
   Create new tmux session with Claude panes
   Returns: {"success": true, "session": "...", "panes": [...]}

2. --robot-status
   Query all session states
   Returns: {"sessions": {...}, "questions": [...], "system": {...}}

3. --robot-health=SESSION
   Get health metrics for session
   Returns: {"agents": {...}, "alerts": [...]}

4. --robot-activity=PANE_ID
   Get activity state for specific pane
   Returns: {"state": "GENERATING", "velocity": 12.5, "confidence": 0.85}

5. --robot-route=SESSION
   Get recommended pane for new work
   Returns: {"recommendation": {"pane_id": "...", "reason": "..."}}

6. --robot-send --pane=PANE_ID --msg=MESSAGE
   Send message to pane
   Returns: {"status": "ok", "delivered": true}

Implementation
--------------

ntm_driver_start_session()
  - Call ntm --robot-spawn with --cc=$parallel for Claude panes
  - Record pane assignments in state
  - Return session info JSON

ntm_driver_get_session_state()
  - Call ntm --robot-activity for pane
  - Map ntm states to unified states
  - Include wait_info if WAITING

ntm_driver_send_to_session()
  - Call ntm --robot-send with pane and message
  - Verify delivery confirmation

ntm_driver_stream_events()
  - Poll --robot-activity periodically
  - Detect state transitions
  - Call callback on significant events

ntm_driver_stop_session()
  - Use tmux kill-session (ntm manages cleanup)

State Mapping
-------------
ntm state      -> Unified state
GENERATING     -> generating
WAITING        -> waiting (with wait_info)
THINKING       -> thinking
STALLED        -> stalled
ERROR          -> error

Health Monitoring Integration
-----------------------------
Start background health monitor:
  while session active:
    health = ntm --robot-health
    for alert in health.alerts:
      handle_alert(alert)
    sleep 10

Advantages Over Local Driver
----------------------------
- Velocity-based detection (more accurate)
- Health monitoring built-in
- Routing for optimal pane selection
- Better stall detection
- Structured error patterns

Testing
-------
- Mock ntm commands for unit tests
- Integration test with real ntm
- Verify state mapping correct
- Verify health alerts handled

Acceptance Criteria
-------------------
- [ ] All driver interface methods implemented
- [ ] State mapping matches unified schema
- [ ] Health monitoring integrated
- [ ] Routing recommendation used
- [ ] Graceful fallback if ntm unavailable","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-04T15:38:45.086444687-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-04T15:38:45.086444687-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-k1kx","depends_on_id":"bd-jm89","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-04T15:45:11.685975198-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-xdrt","title":"Phase 3: ntm Integration (Robot Mode, Activity Detection)","description":"Phase 3: ntm Integration

Overview
--------
Integrate with ntm (Named Tmux Manager) robot mode for advanced orchestration
capabilities: activity detection, health monitoring, and question routing.

Why ntm?
--------
ntm provides capabilities beyond basic tmux:
- Velocity-based activity detection (chars/sec)
- Health monitoring with error pattern detection
- Robot mode JSON API for programmatic control
- Workflow pipelines for complex multi-step tasks
- Session management and routing

Components
----------

3.1 ntm Driver Implementation
   Implement the unified driver interface using ntm robot mode API:
   - ntm --robot-spawn: Create sessions
   - ntm --robot-status: Query session state
   - ntm --robot-health: Monitor session health
   - ntm --robot-route: Get best pane for new work
   - ntm --robot-send: Deliver messages to sessions

3.2 Activity State Detection
   ntm detects Claude state via output velocity:
   - GENERATING: velocity > 10 chars/sec (active output)
   - WAITING: velocity < 1 chars/sec + prompt pattern
   - THINKING: low velocity, no prompt
   - STALLED: velocity == 0 for > 30 seconds
   - ERROR: error patterns detected

3.3 Wait Reason Extraction
   When WAITING, determine WHY:
   - ask_user_question: AskUserQuestion tool detected
   - agent_question_text: Question in plain text
   - external_prompt: Git/SSH/auth prompt
   - unknown: At prompt, reason unclear

3.4 Health Monitoring
   Monitor session health continuously:
   - Detect rate limits (429, quota exceeded)
   - Detect crashes (panic, SIGSEGV)
   - Detect stalls (no output for extended period)
   - Trigger recovery actions

3.5 Workflow Pipeline
   Define github-review.yaml workflow:
   - verify_prerequisites: Check gh auth
   - understand_codebase: Create/update digest
   - review_issues_prs: Main review work
   - finalize_artifacts: Validate plan created

3.6 Rate-Limit Governor
   Adaptive concurrency based on real limits:
   - Query GitHub API for remaining quota
   - Detect model rate limits from 429s
   - Adjust parallelism dynamically
   - Circuit breaker for cascading failures

Exit Criteria
-------------
- ntm driver passes all interface tests
- Activity detection works reliably
- Wait reasons extracted correctly
- Health monitoring catches common issues
- Rate-limit governor adjusts concurrency

Estimated Effort
----------------
~400 lines Bash + ~300 lines in ntm (Go)

-{"id":"bd-z89z","title":"Implement atomic state persistence with flock","description":"# Task: Implement Atomic State Persistence

## Purpose
Store review state (outcomes, questions, runs) in JSON files with atomic writes and flock-based locking to prevent corruption from concurrent access or interrupted writes.

## Background: Why Atomic Writes?
- Ctrl+C during write = corrupted JSON
- Concurrent reads during write = partial data
- Power failure = lost state
- Solution: write to temp file, then atomic mv

## State Files

### 1. review-state.json
```json
{
  "version": 2,
  "repos": {
    "owner/repo": {
      "last_review": "2025-01-04T10:30:00Z",
      "last_review_run_id": "abc123",
      "issues_reviewed": 3,
      "prs_reviewed": 1,
      "issues_resolved": 2,
      "outcome": "completed",
      "duration_seconds": 847,
      "digest_hash": "sha256:..."
    }
  },
  "items": {
    "owner/repo#issue-42": {
      "type": "issue",
      "number": 42,
      "last_review": "2025-01-04T10:30:00Z",
      "outcome": "fixed",
      "notes": "Path handling fixed for Windows"
    }
  },
  "runs": {
    "abc123": {
      "started_at": "2025-01-04T10:00:00Z",
      "completed_at": "2025-01-04T11:30:00Z",
      "repos_processed": 8,
      "items_processed": 14,
      "questions_asked": 12,
      "mode": "ntm"
    }
  }
}
```

### 2. review-questions.json
```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "q_abc123",
      "run_id": "run_xyz",
      "repo": "owner/repo",
      "session_id": "ru-review-owner-repo",
      "pane_id": "1",
      "context": "Should I refactor...",
      "options": ["a) Minimal fix", "b) Full refactor"],
      "priority": "normal",
      "detected_at": "2025-01-04T10:45:00Z",
      "status": "pending"
    }
  ]
}
```

## Implementation

### State Lock
```bash
# Global state lock (separate from review session lock)
STATE_LOCK_FD=201

acquire_state_lock() {
    local lock_file="$RU_STATE_DIR/state.lock"
    exec 201>"$lock_file"
    flock -x 201
}

release_state_lock() {
    flock -u 201 2>/dev/null || true
}

# Scoped lock helper
with_state_lock() {
    acquire_state_lock
    "$@"
    local rc=$?
    release_state_lock
    return $rc
}
```

### Atomic JSON Write
```bash
write_json_atomic() {
    local file="$1"
    local content="$2"
    local tmp_file="${file}.tmp.$$"
    
    # Write to temp file
    echo "$content" > "$tmp_file"
    
    # Validate JSON before committing
    if ! jq empty "$tmp_file" 2>/dev/null; then
        rm -f "$tmp_file"
        log_error "Invalid JSON, refusing to write: $file"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Atomic move
    mv "$tmp_file" "$file"
}
```

### Read with Lock
```bash
read_state_json() {
    local file="$1"
    local default="${2:-{}}"
    
    acquire_state_lock
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        cat "$file"
    else
        echo "$default"
    fi
    release_state_lock
}
```

### Update State
```bash
update_review_state() {
    local updates="$1"  # jq filter
    local state_file="$RU_STATE_DIR/review-state.json"
    
    acquire_state_lock
    
    local current
    if [[ -f "$state_file" ]]; then
        current=$(cat "$state_file")
    else
        current='{"version":2,"repos":{},"items":{},"runs":{}}'
    fi
    
    local updated
    updated=$(echo "$current" | jq "$updates")
    
    write_json_atomic "$state_file" "$updated"
    
    release_state_lock
}
```

### Record Item Outcome
```bash
record_item_outcome() {
    local repo_id="$1"
    local item_type="$2"
    local number="$3"
    local outcome="$4"
    local notes="$5"
    
    local item_key="${repo_id}#${item_type}-${number}"
    local now
    now=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)
    
    update_review_state "
        .items[\"$item_key\"] = {
            \"type\": \"$item_type\",
            \"number\": $number,
            \"last_review\": \"$now\",
            \"outcome\": \"$outcome\",
            \"notes\": $(echo "$notes" | jq -R .)
        }
    "
}
```

## Checkpoint State (for Resume)
```bash
checkpoint_review_state() {
    local checkpoint_file="$RU_STATE_DIR/review-checkpoint.json"
    
    local state
    state=$(cat << EOF
{
  "version": 1,
  "timestamp": "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)",
  "run_id": "$REVIEW_RUN_ID",
  "mode": "$REVIEW_MODE",
  "repos_total": ${#REVIEW_REPOS[@]},
  "repos_completed": ${#COMPLETED_REPOS[@]},
  "repos_pending": $(printf '%s\n' "${PENDING_REPOS[@]}" | jq -R . | jq -s .),
  "questions_pending": $(cat "$RU_STATE_DIR/review-questions.json" 2>/dev/null || echo '{"questions":[]}')
}
EOF
)
    
    with_state_lock write_json_atomic "$checkpoint_file" "$state"
}
```

## Cleanup Old State
```bash
cleanup_old_review_state() {
    local max_age_days="${1:-30}"
    local state_dir="$RU_STATE_DIR"
    
    # Clean old worktrees
    find "$state_dir/worktrees" -maxdepth 1 -type d -mtime "+$max_age_days" \
        -exec rm -rf {} \; 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Prune old runs from state file
    local cutoff
    cutoff=$(date -u -d "$max_age_days days ago" +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ 2>/dev/null || \
             date -u -v-${max_age_days}d +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)
    
    update_review_state "
        .runs |= with_entries(select(.value.started_at > \"$cutoff\"))
    "
}
```

## Testing
- Write, crash, verify temp file cleaned up
- Concurrent writes don't corrupt
- Read during write gets consistent data
- Checkpoint and resume works
- Malformed JSON rejected

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All state writes are atomic (temp + mv)
- [ ] flock prevents concurrent write corruption
- [ ] JSON validated before commit
- [ ] Checkpoint captures full state
- [ ] Old state cleaned up automatically","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-04T15:18:52.733473052-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-04T15:18:52.733473052-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-z89z","depends_on_id":"bd-mnu9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-04T15:44:52.911714994-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-zki","title":"Test task","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-03T16:08:45.034918173-05:00","updated_at":"2026-01-03T16:15:28.281051598-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-03T16:15:28.281051598-05:00","close_reason":"Test issue - removing"}
 {"id":"bd-zlws","title":"Implement git worktree preparation for isolated reviews","description":"# Task: Implement Git Worktree Preparation

## Purpose
Create isolated git worktrees for each repo being reviewed, so AI agents can make changes without affecting the main working directory.

## Background: Why Worktrees?
- Main repo stays untouched until explicit apply
- Easy rollback: just delete the worktree
- Parallel reviews don't conflict
- Clear separation of concerns
- No risk of polluting uncommitted work

## Implementation Details

### prepare_review_worktrees()
```bash
prepare_review_worktrees() {
    local repos=("$@")
    local base="$RU_STATE_DIR/worktrees/$REVIEW_RUN_ID"
    mkdir -p "$base"

    for repo_info in "${repos[@]}"; do
        local repo_spec issues prs updated_at oldest
        IFS='|' read -r repo_spec issues prs updated_at oldest <<< "$repo_info"

        local url branch custom_name local_path repo_id
        resolve_repo_spec "$repo_spec" "$PROJECTS_DIR" "$LAYOUT" \
            url branch custom_name local_path repo_id

        # CRITICAL: Refuse to run on dirty trees
        ensure_clean_or_fail "$local_path"

        # Create worktree path (sanitize repo_id for filesystem)
        local wt_path="$base/${repo_id//\//_}"
        local wt_branch="ru/review/$REVIEW_RUN_ID/${repo_id//\//-}"

        # Fetch latest from remote
        git -C "$local_path" fetch --quiet 2>/dev/null || true

        # Respect branch pins from repo spec
        local base_ref="${branch:-HEAD}"
        
        # Create worktree with new branch
        git -C "$local_path" worktree add -b "$wt_branch" "$wt_path" "$base_ref" >/dev/null

        # Create .ru directory for artifacts
        mkdir -p "$wt_path/.ru"

        # Record mapping for later phases
        record_worktree_mapping "$repo_id" "$wt_path" "$wt_branch"
        
        log_verbose "Created worktree: $repo_id → $wt_path"
    done
}
```

### ensure_clean_or_fail()
```bash
ensure_clean_or_fail() {
    local repo_path="$1"
    
    if ! is_git_repo "$repo_path"; then
        log_error "$repo_path is not a git repository"
        return 1
    fi
    
    local status
    status=$(git -C "$repo_path" status --porcelain 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [[ -n "$status" ]]; then
        log_error "Repository has uncommitted changes: $repo_path"
        log_error "Please commit or stash changes before running review"
        return 1
    fi
    
    return 0
}
```

### record_worktree_mapping()
```bash
record_worktree_mapping() {
    local repo_id="$1"
    local wt_path="$2"
    local wt_branch="$3"
    
    local mapping_file="$RU_STATE_DIR/worktrees/$REVIEW_RUN_ID/mapping.json"
    
    # Initialize if doesn't exist
    [[ ! -f "$mapping_file" ]] && echo '{}' > "$mapping_file"
    
    # Add mapping atomically
    local tmp_file="${mapping_file}.tmp.$$"
    jq --arg repo "$repo_id" \
       --arg path "$wt_path" \
       --arg branch "$wt_branch" \
       '.[$repo] = {"path": $path, "branch": $branch}' \
       "$mapping_file" > "$tmp_file"
    mv "$tmp_file" "$mapping_file"
}
```

### get_worktree_mapping()
```bash
get_worktree_mapping() {
    local repo_info="$1"
    local -n _repo_id=$2
    local -n _wt_path=$3
    
    # Extract repo_id from repo_info
    _repo_id="${repo_info%%|*}"
    
    local mapping_file="$RU_STATE_DIR/worktrees/$REVIEW_RUN_ID/mapping.json"
    _wt_path=$(jq -r --arg repo "$_repo_id" '.[$repo].path // ""' "$mapping_file")
}
```

### cleanup_review_worktrees()
```bash
cleanup_review_worktrees() {
    local run_id="${1:-$REVIEW_RUN_ID}"
    local base="$RU_STATE_DIR/worktrees/$run_id"
    
    [[ ! -d "$base" ]] && return 0
    
    local mapping_file="$base/mapping.json"
    
    if [[ -f "$mapping_file" ]]; then
        # Remove each worktree properly
        while IFS= read -r repo_id; do
            local wt_path
            wt_path=$(jq -r --arg repo "$repo_id" '.[$repo].path' "$mapping_file")
            
            if [[ -d "$wt_path" ]]; then
                # Find the main repo and remove worktree
                local main_repo
                main_repo=$(dirname "$(dirname "$wt_path")")  # heuristic
                git -C "$main_repo" worktree remove --force "$wt_path" 2>/dev/null || \
                    rm -rf "$wt_path"
            fi
        done < <(jq -r 'keys[]' "$mapping_file")
    fi
    
    rm -rf "$base"
}
```

## Directory Structure
```
~/.local/state/ru/worktrees/
└── 20250104-103000-12345/          # Run ID
    ├── mapping.json                 # Repo → worktree mapping
    ├── owner_repo1/                 # Worktree for owner/repo1
    │   ├── .git                     # Worktree git link
    │   ├── .ru/                     # Artifacts directory
    │   │   ├── review-plan.json     # Plan artifact
    │   │   ├── repo-digest.md       # Cached understanding
    │   │   └── session.log          # Session transcript
    │   └── [repo files]
    └── owner_repo2/
        └── ...
```

## Branch Naming Convention
```
ru/review/{RUN_ID}/{repo-id}
ru/review/20250104-103000-12345/owner-repo
```

## Edge Cases
- Repo doesn't exist locally: Skip with warning (or auto-clone?)
- Repo is not a git repo: Error and skip
- Worktree already exists: Error (shouldn't happen with unique run IDs)
- Disk full: Proper error message

## Testing
- Create worktree, verify isolation
- Make changes in worktree, verify main is unchanged
- Cleanup removes worktrees properly
- Branch naming is correct
- Mapping file is accurate

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Worktrees created in correct location
- [ ] Dirty repos rejected with clear message
- [ ] Branch pins from repo spec respected
- [ ] .ru directory created for artifacts
- [ ] Mapping file tracks all worktrees
- [ ] Cleanup removes worktrees and branches","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-04T15:18:52.480379897-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-04T15:18:52.480379897-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-zlws","depends_on_id":"bd-5jph","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-04T15:44:52.892506308-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
